categories.title: ANIMAL ANATOMY
categories.theme: Facts about the physical characteristics or body parts of animals — answer is the ANIMAL, BODY PART, or MATERIAL described

clues.clue: This is what a baby bat is called, a term it shares with young dogs and seals.
Human nails and rhino horns are both made of this tough structural protein.
Snakes and lizards flick their tongues primarily to perform this function.
An adult rabbit carries this many teeth — more than you might expect from a small herbivore.
Unlike most other salamanders, this specific feature on a newt is distinctly flat rather than rounded.

categories.title: WHAT'S MY NAME?
categories.theme: Scientific names, classifications, or Latin terms for animals — answer is the ANIMAL or its SCIENTIFIC DESIGNATION

clues.clue: 'Hippocampus' is the Latin genus name for this small, upright-swimming marine creature.
This speedy spotted cat, the fastest land animal, carries the scientific name Acinonyx jubatus.
The family Hyaenidae belongs to this scientific suborder, placing hyenas closer to cats than dogs.
The aardwolf, despite its name, belongs to this family — the same as spotted and striped hyenas.
The Western Lowland Gorilla holds the unusual distinction of having the same word repeated three times in its full scientific trinomial name.

finals.title: DEEP SEA DWELLERS
finals.clue: Named for the Greek word for 'bottomless,' this zone of the ocean — deeper than 4,000 meters — is the permanent home of abyssopelagic creatures.
finals.answer: At the bottom of the ocean

finals.title: HUNGRY OTTERS
finals.clue: To maintain their high metabolism in cold Pacific waters, sea otters must consume this remarkable fraction of their own body weight in food every single day.
